title: Defense nominee Hegseth faces congressional scrutiny over Iran war costs and civilian deaths
summary: Defense Secretary nominee Pete Hegseth faced intense congressional questioning over US-Iran war costs, civilian casualties, and the legal 60-day war authorization timeline. He claimed the limit paused during the current ceasefire.
contents:
# Hegseth Faces Congressional Scrutiny on Iran War Authorization

## Hearing Highlights
Defense Secretary nominee Pete Hegseth appeared before Congress on May 1, 2026, facing pointed questioning on multiple aspects of the US-Iran military engagement.

## Key Exchanges
- Hegseth claimed the legal 60-day War Powers Resolution limit paused during the current ceasefire period
- A senior Democrat accused Hegseth of misleading President Trump on the war's progress
- Congressional members pressed on civilian death toll and war costs
- A Nevada senator called Hegseth an "antisemite" during proceedings without refutation

## Legal Questions
The 60-day war authorization timeline under the War Powers Resolution emerged as a central point of contention. Critics argue the ceasefire pause interpretation stretches constitutional boundaries.

## Political Risk Analysis
Eurasia Group's Ian Bremmer notes the hearing reflects deepening domestic political divisions over the Iran campaign. The confrontational tone signals potential challenges for defense policy continuity.
